plicable. Before adopting any par
ticular process of concentration,
careful experiments and practical
working tests must be made in the
laboratories, on samples of aver
age ore OF sufficient quantity.


Generally, however, it may be
stated that when ore of payable
value can be cheaply mined on a
large scale, and cheaply transport
ed, the best results are obtained
by shipment in bulk to the smelt
ers, from which special rates can
be obtained, if the mine owner is
able to contract for large and regu
lar supplies of ore. BY shipping
in bulk the following advantages
are derived:


The capital cost, and time neces-
sary to erect and operate a com
plete smelling plant, and i'll add
ton thereto a complete crushing
and concentrating plant is entirely
saved.


Unless ores are specially suit
able for concentration, the losses
in fine shines and in the taillngs
from concentration plants may
vary from Il per cent to 90 per
cent of the original value.


The concentration, which has
now cost so much to produce, must
then be shipped in sacks and han
died at higher freight rates, wheth
er it is smelted locally, or whether
it is loaded, transported and smelt
ed. The samplins of wet concen
trates is liable to be erratic. as it is
, impractiCable to dry and mix them
properly. In addition 10 this, being
finely crushed, the smelter, on re
ceipt Of them, has to "briquette'
them with some binding material
to prevent the fines being carried
away into the flues. The cost OF
briquetting adds from S1.00 per tor
and upwards to the cost OF treat
ment, hence, as above mentioned
il is better TO arrange to ship II
bulk wherever it is practicable.
